selective_codes.theory: Traditional knowledge systems can be understood as economies of transmission investment in which communities allocate finite cognitive, social, and temporal resources toward the reproduction of knowledge domains according to their perceived contribution to livelihood security and cultural identity. Dominant subsistence modes attract disproportionate transmission investment — formalized apprenticeship, specialist roles, ceremonial reinforcement — while secondary domains receive residual or informal transmission. This investment differential produces predictable patterns of knowledge depth, elaboration, and retention across domains within the same community, with dominant domains exhibiting greater taxonomic complexity, more refined operational knowledge, and stronger intergenerational fidelity. Changes in ecological or economic conditions that alter the relative productivity of subsistence domains trigger corresponding reallocation of transmission investment, driving knowledge system evolution.

selective_codes.code_ja: 生態的異質性と知識多様化の連関
selective_codes.theory: Ecological heterogeneity — the co-presence of multiple resource types, habitat patches, and seasonal variability within a community's territory — is the primary material condition driving the diversification and integration of knowledge domains within traditional knowledge systems. Communities inhabiting ecologically heterogeneous environments face adaptive pressure to develop competency across multiple subsistence domains simultaneously, producing complementary rather than hierarchically dominant knowledge configurations. The degree of knowledge diversification therefore serves as an indirect proxy for the ecological diversity of the inhabited landscape, with maximum diversification occurring in communities at the intersection of multiple biomes or resource zones. This nexus between heterogeneity and diversification suggests that knowledge system architecture is ultimately a cognitive map of ecological opportunity structures shaped by long-term co-habitation.

selective_codes.code_ja: 知識体系の境界維持メカニズム
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous traditional knowledge systems maintain their distinctiveness and internal coherence through active boundary mechanisms — social, institutional, and ecological — that regulate knowledge access, transmission eligibility, and domain recognition. Gendered transmission structures, lineage-based craft inheritance, and biome-specific specialization all function as boundary-maintaining processes that prevent the dissolution of knowledge configurations into undifferentiated generic ecological knowledge. These boundaries are socially produced and require ongoing institutional investment to sustain: where social institutions weaken, knowledge boundaries become permeable and domain-specific expertise may be lost or homogenized. The persistence of distinct knowledge system configurations across generations therefore reflects not only ecological continuity but the active reproduction of social mechanisms that define and defend knowledge domain boundaries.

selective_codes.code_ja: 生業様式による知識ヒエラルキー
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous traditional knowledge systems are structurally organized around dominant subsistence modes that create hierarchies of knowledge value, transmission investment, and institutional recognition. The productive mode that most reliably meets caloric and material needs — whether agriculture, pastoralism, or wild resource procurement — commands the greatest share of intergenerational knowledge transmission resources. This hierarchy is not merely reflective of ecological conditions but actively reproduces social arrangements that privilege certain knowledge domains while marginalizing others. The result is a structured knowledge economy in which expertise is differentially valorized according to its proximity to the dominant subsistence mode.

selective_codes.code_ja: 生態的ニッチによる知識専門化の勾配
selective_codes.theory: The degree and direction of traditional knowledge specialization follows a gradient determined by the ecological niche a community inhabits over generational time. Biome-specific conditions — arctic tundra, tropical rainforest, maritime island, arid steppe — function as selection pressures that concentrate knowledge development in domains most responsive to locally available and seasonally reliable resources. Communities in ecologically homogeneous environments develop narrower but deeper specialization, while those in heterogeneous landscapes develop broader multi-domain expertise. This gradient constitutes a fundamental organizing principle through which ecological context and knowledge architecture co-evolve.

selective_codes.code_ja: 知識領域統合のアーキテクチャ
selective_codes.theory: Traditional knowledge systems exhibit identifiable architectures of domain integration ranging from hegemonic monopolization, through asymmetric weighting, to complementary balance among multiple subsistence domains. This architecture reflects not only ecological affordances but also the social institutions — exchange networks, ceremonial obligations, kinship structures — that govern how knowledge domains are relationally organized. Where no single subsistence mode overwhelmingly dominates, communities develop institutional mechanisms that formally recognize and transmit multiple domains as structurally interdependent. The architecture of integration therefore encodes both ecological history and social organization within the knowledge system itself.

selective_codes.code_ja: 生産集約化による知識の変位
selective_codes.theory: Transitions toward more intensive productive systems — particularly sedentary agriculture — systematically displace and marginalize pre-existing knowledge domains, most notably wild resource gathering expertise. As communities redirect cognitive and social resources toward higher-yield productive strategies, gathering knowledge is progressively relegated to supplementary or residual status, reducing its transmission fidelity across generations. This displacement process is not merely additive but involves active devaluation: knowledge domains that once held structural centrality are reconstituted as peripheral, low-prestige supplements to dominant productive modes. The intensity of displacement correlates with the degree of productive specialization and the availability of cultivable or heritable land.

selective_codes.code_ja: 環境と知識の相互構成
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous traditional knowledge systems and the ecological environments in which they are embedded are mutually constitutive: environmental conditions shape knowledge configuration while knowledge practices actively shape environmental outcomes. Communities dwelling in ecologically complex or heterogeneous landscapes develop integrative multi-domain knowledge systems that mirror landscape heterogeneity, creating a structural homology between environmental and knowledge organization. Long-term inhabitation of a specific biome produces not merely accumulated information but a distinctive epistemological orientation — a way of perceiving and categorizing ecological relations — that is inseparable from the environment itself. The loss of ecological context therefore entails not just knowledge erosion but the dissolution of the relational conditions that make the knowledge intelligible.

selective_codes.code_ja: 知識周辺化の構造的過程
selective_codes.theory: The marginalization of specific knowledge domains within indigenous systems is a structural process produced by the intersection of ecological opportunity, productive hierarchy, and social valuation rather than a simple reflection of declining utility. Gathering knowledge — and by extension other non-dominant subsistence knowledge forms — occupies a peripheralized position that is actively maintained through differential transmission investment, prestige allocation, and institutional neglect. This peripheralization is partial and graduated: knowledge domains may retain moderate recognized status even when structurally subordinated, reflecting the persistence of ecological conditions that sustain their residual utility. The structural process of peripheralization thus generates a spectrum of knowledge status positions rather than a binary dominant/absent distinction.

selective_codes.code_ja: 水域・大陸的知識体系の分岐
selective_codes.theory: A fundamental divergence in the architecture of traditional knowledge systems separates communities whose primary subsistence orientation is directed toward aquatic and maritime resources from those organized around continental terrestrial modes of agriculture, pastoralism, or terrestrial foraging. Aquatic and island-based knowledge systems develop around tidal, migratory, and oceanic rhythms that generate distinctive seasonal calendars, navigational expertise, and species-specific procurement knowledge that have no structural equivalent in terrestrial systems. This divergence is not merely technical but ontological: the relational categories through which communities understand resource availability, ecosystem behavior, and sustainable management differ fundamentally between aquatic and terrestrial orientations. Maritime and insular knowledge systems therefore represent a qualitatively distinct knowledge configuration rather than a variant of terrestrial patterns.

selective_codes.code_ja: コミュニティ間知識ハイブリッド化のダイナミクス
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous traditional knowledge systems are not closed, bounded repositories but dynamic systems continuously reconfigured through inter-community knowledge exchange facilitated by social institutions of marriage, trade, and ceremonial alliance. Affinal networks serve as the primary infrastructure for knowledge hybridization, enabling the crossing of ecological zone boundaries and the recombination of domain-specific expertise that no single community could develop in isolation. The directionality and content of hybridization are structurally governed by the ecological complementarity of intermarrying communities and the residence rules that determine where new knowledge is practiced and transmitted. Hybridization thus produces knowledge systems whose configuration reflects accumulated social history — the record of alliances, migrations, and ecological encounters — as much as current environmental conditions.

selective_codes.code_ja: 野生資源知識の残余的持続
selective_codes.theory: Wild resource and gathering knowledge demonstrates a pattern of residual persistence within intensively agricultural or pastoral communities, maintaining a structural presence — however marginal — that reflects the continued ecological availability of gatherable resources even under conditions of productive intensification. This persistence is not merely vestigial but functionally significant: gathering knowledge serves as a risk-buffering system activated during agricultural failure, seasonal scarcity, or economic disruption, giving it adaptive value disproportionate to its everyday transmission priority. The graduated spectrum from moderate contribution through marginal supplementation suggests that wild resource knowledge occupies a permanently available but variably activated position within knowledge systems rather than being either fully integrated or fully lost. This residual persistence constitutes a form of knowledge resilience embedded in the ecological relationship between communities and their non-cultivated environments.

selective_codes.code_ja: 社会的ネットワークによる知識循環経路
selective_codes.theory: Indigenous traditional knowledge circulates across community boundaries through structured social pathways — primarily affinal and kinship networks — that determine both the direction and content of inter-community knowledge transfer. Marriage institutions, residence rules, and exchange obligations create predictable vectors along which specialized ecological and craft knowledge moves from one knowledge community to another, enabling hybridization and adaptive recombination. The direction of knowledge flow is not random but is governed by structural asymmetries in social relations, including the relative ecological or economic prestige of knowledge-sending versus knowledge-receiving communities. Atypical reversals of these flows — where knowledge returns from affinal to natal communities — signal conditions of unusual ecological complementarity or social reconfiguration.

selective_codes.code_ja: 知識伝達インフラとしてのジェンダー
selective_codes.theory: Gender functions not merely as a social category but as a structural infrastructure through which specialized craft and technical knowledge is transmitted, preserved, and bounded within indigenous communities. The gender-based division of productive labor creates distinct transmission channels — patrilineal, matrilineal, or matrifocal — that operate semi-autonomously, preserving specialized expertise within socially defined practitioner communities. This infrastructure simultaneously ensures knowledge continuity by stabilizing transmission pathways and constrains knowledge diffusion by limiting cross-gender access. The gendering of knowledge transmission is therefore both a conservation mechanism and a boundary-maintaining process that shapes the overall architecture of the knowledge system.

selective_codes.code_ja: 牧畜知識体系の自律性
selective_codes.theory: Pastoral knowledge systems constitute an autonomous and structurally distinctive configuration of traditional ecological knowledge that is not reducible to agricultural or foraging variants, arising from the specific demands of managing mobile or semi-mobile domesticated animal populations across extensive range environments. The primacy of animal behavior, pasture ecology, veterinary knowledge, and seasonal mobility routes in pastoral systems creates a knowledge architecture organized around spatial and temporal movement rather than the place-based sedentary orientation characteristic of agricultural systems. This structural autonomy means that pastoral knowledge communities develop unique knowledge transmission mechanisms — particularly oral and performative traditions suited to mobile lifestyles — that differ in form as well as content from settled agricultural knowledge transmission. The ecological marginality of pastoral habitats, rather than being a limitation, becomes the generative condition that necessitates and sustains this distinctive knowledge configuration.

top_axial_codes.code_ja: 採集知識の周辺的地位

牧畜知識体系の優位性

samples.title: Settlement patterns: Segmented communities, i.e., those divided into barrios, wards, or hamlets, each of which is essentially a localized kin group, a clan or ramage, in the absence of any indication of local exogamy. Large extended families (see "Domestic organization"), are treated as clan-barrios if they are integrated by a rule of ambilineal, matrilineal, or patrilineal descent.

data_sources.name: D-PLACE (Database of Places, Language, Culture and Environment)
data_sources.ethical_notes: Academic ethnographic data. Compiled from published sources with varying consent histories.

data_sources.name: UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage
data_sources.ethical_notes: Fully public. All elements inscribed with community consent (FPIC compliant). Safest source.

data_sources.name: Standard Cross-Cultural Sample (SCCS)
data_sources.ethical_notes: Academic data from Murdock & White (1969). 186 representative societies, ~2,000 variables.

data_sources.name: Binford Hunter-Gatherer Dataset
data_sources.ethical_notes: Compiled by Lewis Binford from ethnographic literature. Focus on subsistence ecology.

data_sources.name: Ethnographic Atlas
data_sources.ethical_notes: George P. Murdock (1967). 1,291 societies. Integrated into D-PLACE.
